coredata.dc:title: Synthesis-identification integration: One-pot hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for differentiating nucleobases with the aid of multivariate chemometrics analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuang, Qianfen
Cao, Wei
Ni, Yongnian
Wang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Most of the conventional multidimensional differential sensors currently need at least two-step fabrication, namely synthesis of probe(s) and identification of multiple analytes by mixing of analytes with probe(s), and were conducted using multiple sensing elements or several devices. In the study, we chose five different nucleobases (adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine, and uracil) as model analytes, and found that under hydrothermal conditions, sodium citrate could react directly with various nucleobases to yield different nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs). The CDs synthesized from different nucleobases exhibited different fluorescent properties, leading to their respective characteristic fluorescence spectra. Hence, we combined the fluorescence spectra of the CDs with advanced chemometrics like principle component analysis (PCA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), K-nearest neighbor (KNN) and soft independent modeling of class analogy (SIMCA), to present a conceptually novel “synthesis-identification integration” strategy to construct a multidimensional differential sensor for nucleobase discrimination. Single-wavelength excitation fluorescence spectral data, single-wavelength emission fluorescence spectral data, and fluorescence Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs) of the CDs were respectively used as input data of the differential sensor. The results showed that the discrimination ability of the multidimensional differential sensor with EEM data set as input data was superior to those with single-wavelength excitation/emission fluorescence data set, suggesting that increasing the number of the data input could improve the discrimination power. Two supervised pattern recognition methods, namely KNN and SIMCA, correctly identified the five nucleobases with a classification accuracy of 100%. The proposed “synthesis-identification integration” strategy together with a multidimensional array of experimental data holds great promise in the construction of differential sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis-identification integration
Differential sensor
Carbon nanodots
Multivariate chemometrics
Nucleobases
Excitation-Emission Matrices

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for multidimensional differential sensing of nucleobases
Characterization of fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Discrimination of five nucleobases using advanced chemometrics analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Contour map of the three-dimensional fluorescence EEM spectra of (a) CDs-adenine, (b) CDs-cytosine, (c) CDs-guanine, (d) CDs-thymine, (e) CDs-uracil.

Left: two-dimensional PCA score plot, showing PC2 versus PC1 illustrating the ability of the differential sensor to discriminate five nucleobases at a concentration of 37.5 mM; Right: eigenanalysis from different data sources, showing the eigenvalue of the first five PCs, and the corresponding proportion and cumulative proportion of variance accounted for by each component. Three different fluorescence data sets of the CDs were respectively used as the input data of the differential sensor: (a) single-wavelength excitation spectral data, (b) single-wavelength emission spectral data, (c) fluorescence EEMs.

Two-dimensional PCA loading plot, showing the first two loadings versus variable. Three different fluorescence data sets of the CDs were respectively used as the input data of the differential sensor: (a) single-wavelength excitation spectral data, (b) single-wavelength emission spectral data, (c) fluorescence EEMs.

HCA dendrogram for 35 samples obtained from five nucleobases at a concentration of 37.5 mM; A: sample No. 1–7; C: sample No. 8–14; G: sample No. 15–21; T: sample No. 22–28; U: sample No. 29–35. Single-wavelength excitation spectral data sets of the CDs were used as the input data of the differential sensor.

HCA dendrogram for 35 samples obtained from five nucleobases at a concentration of 37.5 mM; A: sample No. 1–7; C: sample No. 8–14; G: sample No. 15–21; T: sample No. 22–28; U: sample No. 29–35. Single-wavelength emission spectral data sets of the CDs were used as the input data of the differential sensor.

HCA dendrogram for 35 samples obtained from five nucleobases at a concentration of 37.5 mM; A: sample No. 1–7; C: sample No. 8–14; G: sample No. 15–21; T: sample No. 22–28; U: sample No. 29–35. Fluorescence EEMs data sets of the CDs were used as the input data of the differential sensor.

Schematic illustration of “synthesis-identification integration” strategy coupled with a multidimensional array of fluorescence EEM data to construct a multidimensional differential sensor for the discrimination of nucleobases.
Classification results obtained by the KNN and SIMCA models.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The numbers of PCs selected in each PCA models were 3, 3, 4, 3, 2, respectively.

head.ce:title: Synthesis-identification integration: One-pot hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for differentiating nucleobases with the aid of multivariate chemometrics analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most of the conventional multidimensional differential sensors currently need at least two-step fabrication, namely synthesis of probe(s) and identification of multiple analytes by mixing of analytes with probe(s), and were conducted using multiple sensing elements or several devices. In the study, we chose five different nucleobases (adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine, and uracil) as model analytes, and found that under hydrothermal conditions, sodium citrate could react directly with various nucleobases to yield different nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs). The CDs synthesized from different nucleobases exhibited different fluorescent properties, leading to their respective characteristic fluorescence spectra. Hence, we combined the fluorescence spectra of the CDs with advanced chemometrics like principle component analysis (PCA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), K-nearest neighbor (KNN) and soft independent modeling of class analogy (SIMCA), to present a conceptually novel “synthesis-identification integration” strategy to construct a multidimensional differential sensor for nucleobase discrimination. Single-wavelength excitation fluorescence spectral data, single-wavelength emission fluorescence spectral data, and fluorescence Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs) of the CDs were respectively used as input data of the differential sensor. The results showed that the discrimination ability of the multidimensional differential sensor with EEM data set as input data was superior to those with single-wavelength excitation/emission fluorescence data set, suggesting that increasing the number of the data input could improve the discrimination power. Two supervised pattern recognition methods, namely KNN and SIMCA, correctly identified the five nucleobases with a classification accuracy of 100%. The proposed “synthesis-identification integration” strategy together with a multidimensional array of experimental data holds great promise in the construction of differential sensors.

A novel “synthesis-identification integration” strategy coupled with multivariate chemometrics methods was developed to fabricate a multidimensional differential sensor for nucleobase discrimination.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Up to date, sensing-based analysis of multianalytes have drawn many interests from researchers due to its applications in a wide variety of areas such as environmental monitoring, food safety, clinical diagnosis, and so on [1–11]. However, the development of sensor(s) with multianalyte capability is still a current major scientific and technological challenge. Many existing sensors depend on the traditional strategy of ‘lock-and-key’ design to realize the identification of analyte(s) [1–6]. The multianalyte sensing mode requires the synthesis of separate, unique and highly selective artificial receptor (“lock”) which can bind each analyte of interest (“key”). Obviously, the synthesis of different selective artificial receptor for individual analyte is always a time- and resource-intensive process, and usually involves complicated functionalization or chemical modification. Also, the detection mode of the one-sensor-per-analyte strategy usually suffers strict experimental conditions. More importantly, most sensors based on the ‘lock-and-key’ recognition mechanism more or less displayed some cross-reactivity to chemically or structurally similar substances.

To alleviate the aforementioned difficulties, differential sensing strategy has recently become a potential alternative to the ‘lock-and-key’ protocol [7–11]. Differential sensing strategy usually employs multiple nonspecific or weakly interacting receptors to fabricate a cross-reactive sensor array. The sensor array uses only a single-channel signal (usually signal intensity) to generate a unique fingerprint pattern in response to each analyte. Then, the large amount of data from the fingerprint pattern is processed by multivariate chemometric pattern recognition algorithms to classify and identify different analytes [7–11]. The conventional single-dimensional differential sensing strategy has been currently challenged by the multidimensional differential sensing strategy which extracts more than one signal from a single sensing element to form a characteristic signal pattern, because the multidimensional sensing strategy has superiority in terms of number of sensing elements, discrimination performance, sensing reproducibility, and sample consumption [12–19]. In general, multidimensional differential sensing platform can be fabricated either by mechanically incorporating several different transducers like colorimetric, fluorescent, electrochemical, and mass-sensitive, on a single-chip microsensor system [13,14], or by extracting multiple signals, such as colorimetric, fluorescent, phosphorescent and so on, from individual sensing element (known as “lab-on-a-molecule” or “lab-on-a-nanoparticle”) [15–19]. The former multidimensional sensing mode needs multiple different devices to detect different signals, resulting in complicated operations, high cost and unsatisfied reproducibility. For the latter multidimensional sensing mode, materials with multidimensional information are rare or complicated for design and chemical synthesis. Hence, finding a less complicated and universal multidimensional differential sensing strategy is always attractive, especially if the strategy is performed only using a single measurement and device.

At present, with the advancement of science and technology, many modern instruments can generate a multidimensional array of experimental data per sample. For example, optical device like spectrophotometry or spectrofluorimetry usually records a UV–Vis or fluorescence spectrum in a single measurement, which is represented as an array of absorbance or fluorescence intensity derived from multiple wavelengths; electrochemical device can record a voltammogram in a single measurement, which is expressed as an array of current at multiple potentials. Obviously, in the sensing process, different analyte usually gives rise to different spectral signature of response signals. Hence, theoretically, a characteristic spectrum collected using a single measurement and device is sufficient as data input to construct a simple, cost-effective, and universal multidimensional differential sensing platform for detection and discrimination of different analytes.

On the other hand, compared with other analytical techniques, fluorescence-based technique has recently showed great potentials for designing a differential sensor because of its simple instrument, ease of operation, and rich optical information [20–27]. However, the application of most differential fluorescence sensors currently needs two steps, namely, (i) synthesis of functional fluorescent probe(s), (ii) identification of different analytes: mixing of analytes and functional fluorescent probe(s) to bring about their interactions which lead to the characteristic fluorescence change of the probe(s) [21–27]. The two above-mentioned steps are rather time-consuming, and the preparation of high-quality functional fluorescent probe(s) usually requires specific separation and purification, which increase the complexity of the differential sensing assay. Therefore, a conceptual demonstration of a differential fluorescence sensor, which simultaneously realizes the synthesis of fluorescent probes(s) and identification of different analytes, will be of great significance for both fundamental research and applications.

In the present study, we described a conceptually novel “synthesis-identification integration” strategy to construct a differential fluorescence sensing platform. The differential sensors can simultaneously realize the synthesis of fluorescent probes(s) and identification of different analytes. We used the strategy for identification of five different nitrogen-containing nucleobases, namely, adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), thymine (T), or uracil (U) (chemical structures: Table 1). These nucleobases are the main ingredients of nucleic acid, and play an important role in the carriers of genetic information and the material basis of genetic expression [28,29]. Therefore, it is appealing to develop a method for recognizing the chemically or structurally similar nucleobases and reporting their presence. Most importantly, the new sensing platform took advantage of a one-step hydrothermal reaction to construct a differential sensor. We found that under hydrothermal conditions, sodium citrate (CANa) could react directly with various nucleobases to produce different nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs). The CDs synthesized from different nucleobases showed different fluorescent properties, resulting in their respective characteristic fluorescence spectra. Herein, we directly utilized the unique spectra to develop a new multidimensional differential sensing strategy for nucleobase discrimination. The strategy only employed a single measurement and device. We measured the three-way fluorescence excitation–emission matrix (EEM) spectra of the sensory system, and represented the EEM spectral data as a multidimensional array of fluorescence intensity at multiple wavelengths. The multidimensional data array was then analyzed using multivariate chemometrics tools including unsupervised pattern recognition (principal component analysis−PCA, hierarchical cluster analysis−HCA) and supervised pattern recognition (K-nearest neighbors−KNN, soft independent modeling of class analogy−SIMCA). The results showed that the developed differential sensor had the strong discrimination ability for the chemically or structurally similar nucleobases, confirming the validity of the new strategy. In addition, we demonstrated that the discrimination ability of the differential sensor could be improved by increasing the number of the data input of fluorescence spectra. The brief schematic for the multidimensional differential sensing of nucleobases was depicted in Scheme 1.

Adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), thymine (T), uracil (U), and trisodium citrate dihydrate (CANa) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on a F-4500 spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi, Japan) with a standard 10-mm path length cell. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images measurements were performed with a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured from KBr window on a Tensor 27 FTIR Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded with a PANalytical X′Pert PRO diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected with an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer from VG Scientific using 300 W Al Kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for multidimensional differential sensing of nucleobases
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, multidimensional differential sensor for nucleobase discrimination was fabricated on the basis of the formation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots. Briefly, 73.5 or 0.98 mg of trisodium citrate dihydrate, a certain amount of nucleobase (final concentration: 37.5 mM or 0.5 mM) and 20 mL of water was sequentially added into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave chamber. After ultrasonication for 5 min, the autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the supernatant containing nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) was collected after centrifuged at 16,000 rpm for 10 min. The resultant solution was used for fluorescence assays. All measurements were taken at room temperature. The fluorescence CDs synthesized using trisodium citrate and adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine or uracil were respectively represented by the abbreviation "CDs-adenine", "CDs-cytosine", "CDs-guanine", "CDs-thymine", "CDs-uracil".

Fluorescence Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs) of five kinds of CDs were obtained by varying the excitation wavelength (λ ex) ranging between 250 and 500 nm (every 10 nm), and recording the emission spectra (λ em) from 260 to 600 (every 5 nm). For these measurements, the widths of the excitation and emission slits were 10 and 10 nm, respectively, and the scan rate was fixed to 12,000 nm min−1. Each EEM had the dimension 69 × 26, with 69 and 26 being respectively the number of emission and excitation wavelengths, and were preprocessed to eliminate the observed Rayleigh and Raman scatterings by using the algorithm proposed by Zepp et al. [30]. Each treated EEM data were then unfolded into a vector (size 1 × 1794). At a given concentration, seven independent measurements were performed for each individual CDs. These responses of the five kinds of CDs using nucleobases as starting materials can be represented as a data set matrix [(7 replicates × 5 nucleobases) × 1794 wavelengths]. The data matrix was autoscaled along the wavelength direction to remove the effect of the fluctuating signal [31–33]. Then, the treated data were submitted to advanced chemometrics analysis such as principal component analysis (PCA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), K-nearest neighbors (KNN), and soft independent modeling of class analogies (SIMCA) [31–33]. All the data treatment and advanced chemometrics analysis were conducted under Matlab 7.0 computer environment.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured according to established procedure [34]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (literature quantum yield is 0.546) was selected as a standard [35]. Absorbance values of samples should not exceed 0.1 in order to minimize the reabsorption effects. The QY was calculated according to Eq. (1) below [34]:
(1)
QY
=
QY
std
×
(
n
2
/
n
2
std
)
×
(
K /
K
std
)
where the subscript std represents quinine sulfate, the absence of the subscript denotes an unknown CDs sample, K denotes the slope of the curve from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance, and n is the refractive index of solvent (for water and a 0.1 M H2SO4, the refractive index is 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the construction of multidimensional differential sensing platform for discrimination of nucleobases depended on the formation of fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs). Thus, we employed various analytical characterization techniques like TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and fluorescence spectra to confirm the formation of CDs.

Fig. S1 showed TEM images of CDs prepared under hydrothermal conditions by using various nucleobases and CANa. The five kinds of CDs were nearly spherical nanoparticles, implying the formation of CDs. By measuring many particles in the TEM image, the average diameter of the five kinds of CDs were approximately 2 nm. XRD was used to further study the internal structure of the as-prepared CDs. As displayed in Fig. S2, XRD pattern of the five CDs had two broad peaks at 2θ of approximately 28° (d = 0.34 nm) and approximately 40° (d = 0.23 nm), which were respectively assigned to (002) and (100) facets of graphite [36]. Both of the wide peaks revealed an amorphous carbon phase of these CDs [36].

The FT-IR of five kinds of CDs are illustrated in Fig. S3. It was clear that the IR of the as-prepared CDs showed a distinct broad absorption band centered around 3460 cm−1, which was assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. For these CDs, a peak located at ca. 1650 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C˭O and C˭N, and a band peaking at ca. 1570 cm−1 was ascribed to a N-H bending vibration. These CDs displayed a peak around 1400 cm−1, which was attributed to the C-N stretching vibration. These results showed that the prepared CDs contained N and O elements, and very likely possessed multiple functional groups like −OH, −NH2, −COOR, and −CONHR.

XPS was further conducted to verify the surface states of the CDs. For all the CDs, the high resolution spectra of the O 1s region can be fitted into three main wide peaks centered at ca. 531.0 eV, 532.0 eV, and 535.5 eV, which were respectively assigned to C˭O, C-O groups, and to H2O (Figs. S4a, S5a, S6a, S7a, and S8a) [37,38]. For all the CDs, deconvolution of the high resolution C1s spectra gave rise to three peaks centered at ca. 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.3 eV, which were assigned to sp2 C in C˭C/C-C, sp3 C in C-O and C-N, and sp2 C in C˭O, respectively (Figs. S4b, S5b, S6b, S7b, and S8b) [39,40]. For CDs-cytosine, CDs-thymine, and CDs-uracil, the high resolution N1s spectra showed a broad band peaking around 400.3 eV, which was attributed to pyrrolic N (C2-N-H)/graphitic N (N-C3) (Figs. S5c, S7c, and S8c) [39,40]. For CDs-adenine and CDs-guanine, the high resolution spectra of the N 1s region can be fitted into two intense peaks centered at ca. 398.9 eV, and 400.3 eV, which corresponded respectively to pyridinic N (C-N-H)/amide N (O˭C-NH), and pyrrolic N (C2-N-H)/graphitic N (N-C3) (Figs. S4c and S6c).

Fig. 1 and S9 respectively showed the contour map of the fluorescence excitation emission matrix (EEM) data of these CDs, and the fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of these CDs at different excitation wavelengths. It can be noted that CDs-cytosine, CDs-guanine, CDs-thymine, and CDs-uracil exhibited fluorescence maxima at 340 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em), 340 nm/435 nm (Ex/Em), 330 nm/380 nm (Ex/Em), 330 nm/430 nm (Ex/Em), respectively. The CDs-adenine showed two fluorescence maxima: one at 380 nm/435 nm (Ex/Em) and a second one at 300 nm/430 nm (Ex/Em). On the basis of the comparative approach with quinine sulfate, the QY of the CDs-adenine, CDs-cytosine, CDs-guanine, CDs-thymine, and CDs-uracil was estimated to be 44.6%, 20.1%, 15.6%, 3.9%, and 3.7%, respectively. The fluorescence QY of CDs prepared by the five nucleobases decreased in the order A>C>G>>T>U. This result was very likely caused by the property of amine group of nucleobase, the electron donating/withdrawing capability of nucleobase, the affinity of nucleobase for carbon nanomaterials and, as well as the nucleobase quenching efficiency [41–47]. Previous literature reported that fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs could be synthesized using sodium citrate or citrate and three types of amines (primary amines, secondary amines and tertiary amines) as the carbon source and nitrogen source, and the primary amine served dual roles as N-doping precursors and surface passivation agents, both of which led to the highest QY among the three types of amines [41–43]. In addition, it was found from earlier literature that there was a positive correlation between the electron donating capability of the nitrogen source and the QY of CDs [41,42]. In the work, the bases A, C and G contained a primary amine group, but the bases T and U did not have such a group. More importantly, the bases T and U had two carbonyl groups (C˭O) with electron withdrawing capability. These meant that the QY of the bases A, C and G should be theoretically far greater than that of the bases T and U. Moreover, the bases C and G contained a C˭O group, and the base T possessed a methyl group (CH3) with electron donating capability. These results suggested that the QY of the base A should be theoretically higher than that of the bases C and G, and the QY of the base T should be theoretically higher than that of the base U. On the basis of the aforementioned analysis, we could suppose that the fluorescence QY of CDs decreased in the order A>C, G>>T>U. Furthermore, some recent reports showed that the base G had the highest affinity for carbon nanomaterials and the strongest quenching efficiency among the five kinds of bases [44–47]. The results implied that the QY of the base G should be theoretically weaker than that of the base C. Therefore, the experimental order of the fluorescence QY of CDs prepared by the five nucleobases agreed well with that theoretical one.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of five nucleobases using advanced chemometrics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Looking at Fig. 1 and S9, we can find that the fluorescence spectra of various CDs overlapped, but various CDs possessed significantly different spectral features, i.e., different spectral shapes, different fluorescence maxima, and different spectral variation trends. The overlapping spectra hindered the direct identification of the five nucleobases. However, these spectral differences, which act as "fingerprints" of nucleobase, can be combined with advanced chemometrics approaches such as PCA, HCA, KNN, and SIMCA, to develop a differential sensing platform for distinguishing between the five nucleobases.

In the study, we firstly collected fluorescence Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs) of thirty-five CDs (7 replicates × 5 nucleobases), and then used the algorithm proposed by Zepp et al. to remove the Rayleigh and Raman scatterings of EEMs [30]. After that, three different combinations of the fluorescence data sets were used as input data. The three different combinations were (i) single-wavelength excitation fluorescence spectral data (ex-340 nm, wavelength range - em: 370–600 nm); (ii) single-wavelength emission fluorescence spectral data (em-435 nm, wavelength range - ex: 250–400 nm); (iii) EEM spectral data (wavelength range - ex: 250–500 nm, em: 260–600 nm). For single-wavelength excitation data set, these fluorescence responses can be represented as a data set matrix [(7 replicates × 5 nucleobases) × 47 wavelengths]. For single-wavelength emission data set, these responses can be expressed as a data set matrix [(7 replicates × 5 nucleobases) × 16 wavelengths]. For EEM spectral data set, these responses can be represented as a data set matrix [(7 replicates × 5 nucleobases) × 1794 wavelengths]. An autoscaling preprocessing procedure was applied for all the data matrix along the wavelength direction, and the treated data were submitted to a well-known unsupervised pattern recognition analysis, namely principal component analysis (PCA) [31–33]. PCA transforms the data matrix into a set of linearly uncorrelated principal components (PCs). The eigenanalysis from different data sources and the first two PC scores can be visualized in a two-dimensional (2D) score plot shown in Fig. 2. It was clear from Fig. 2 that when the concentration of each nucleobase to be discriminated was 37.5 mM, the first two PCs accounted for above 92% of the original data information. At the same time, it was found from Fig. 2a that for single-wavelength excitation spectral data set, three out of five nucleobases were clearly identified using PCA analysis, but two nucleobases (G and C) exhibited significantly overlap. In addition, it was also observed from the 2D score plot that for single-wavelength emission spectral data set, five nucleobases could be identified, but the bases G and C must be carefully differentiated (see the inset in Fig. 2b). These results suggested that single-wavelength excitation or emission data set as input data provided the limited discrimination ability. It was noteworthy that EEM spectral data were employed to improve the discrimination ability of the multidimensional sensor assay (see Fig. 2c). G and C were effectively separated from the other three nucleobases. These results demonstrated that multidimensional differential sensor for nucleobase discrimination using EEM data set as input data was feasible, and increasing the number of the data input of fluorescence spectra could greatly improve the discriminating power. Therefore, EEM spectral data set was used as input data in the following work. On the other hand, the loading plots from PCA analysis were also displayed (see Fig. 3). For single-wavelength excitation (340 nm) spectral data set, the PC1 loading plot showed significant spectral features at the emission wavelength of 385 and 445 nm; the PC2 loading plot was related with two strong features at the emission wavelength of 420 and 475 nm (see Fig. 3a). For single-wavelength emission (435 nm) spectral data set, the PC1 loading plot exhibited noticeable spectral features at the excitation wavelength of 330 and 390 nm; the PC2 loading plot was associated with two intense features at the excitation wavelength of 310 and 350 nm (see Fig. 3b). For EEM spectral data set, the PC1 loading plot had many strong positive peaks like 310 nm/380 nm (Ex/Em), 320 nm/380 nm (Ex/Em), 330 nm/385 nm (Ex/Em), 340 nm/385 nm (Ex/Em), 350 nm/365 nm (Ex/Em) and 360 nm/375 nm (Ex/Em), and many intense negative peaks like 340 nm/445 nm (Ex/Em), 350 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em), 360 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em), 370 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em), 380 nm/435 nm (Ex/Em), 390 nm/430 nm (Ex/Em) and 400 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em); the PC2 loading plot also had many strong positive and negative peaks such as 320 nm/445 nm (Ex/Em), 330 nm/445 nm (Ex/Em), 340 nm/445 nm (Ex/Em), 350 nm/450 nm (Ex/Em), 360 nm/465 nm (Ex/Em), 370 nm/440 nm (Ex/Em), 380 nm/435 nm (Ex/Em), 390 nm/430 nm (Ex/Em), 400 nm/435 nm (Ex/Em) and 410 nm/430 nm (Ex/Em) (see Fig. 3c). On the basis of the aforementioned results, it seemed that EEM spectral data set could exploit many strong spectral features, which were responsible for nucleobase discrimination. Therefore, the loading results further confirmed that the increase in the number of the data input of fluorescence spectra remarkably enhanced the discriminating power.

We then used another unsupervised pattern recognition method, namely HCA, to further test the performance of the multidimensional differential sensor. HCA seeks classification of the samples by measuring the interpoint distances (in this work, the Euclidean distance) between all samples in the n-dimensional space resulting from n-numbers of studied features (in the work, 47 dimensions for single-wavelength excitation data, 16 dimensions for single-wavelength emission data, 1794 dimensions for EEM spectral data) [31–33]. It creates a hierarchy of clusters which is displayed in a tree structure called dendrogram [31–33]. HCA based on the minimum variance method (Ward's method) was shown in Fig. 4. As seen from Fig. 4, the bases G and C could not be correctly identified using single-wavelength excitation fluorescence spectral data as input data of HCA analysis. For single-wavelength emission spectral data set or EEM spectral data set, all the samples were correctly assigned to five distinct groups, which respectively represent five different types of nucleobases, indicating that nucleobase discrimination could be successfully obtained (see
Figs. 5 and 6). All the results were in good agreement with those of PCA analysis. After successful discrimination of the five nucleobases at the concentration of 37.5 mM, the next step was to differentiate the five nucleobases at a low concentration. It was found that when the concentration of each individual nucleobase was lowered to 0.5 mM, five distinct groups could be still produced in the PCA score plot or the HCA dendrogram, denoting the good discrimination ability of nucleobase identification (Figs. S10 and S11).

Two supervised pattern recognition methods, KNN and SIMCA, were applied to classify the 35 nucleobase samples at the concentration of 0.5 mM on the basis of EEM spectral data to ascertain if both approaches could discriminate the five nucleobases. The KNN is a non-parametric data classification method on the basis of the estimation of the Euclidian distances between an unknown object and each of the objects of the training set [31–33]. The nearest K objects are exclusively applied to make any given classification. The assignment of each object to a particular class depend on the shortest estimated distance. SIMCA conducts as many PCA models as input-classes in study, and then, the classification is performed from the distance of the object to the center of each principal component score space [31–33]. The closest distance to the corresponding regions established by the PC model was then employed to judge the class membership. The selection of the optimal number of principal components (PCs) for describing each class is based on a cross-validation procedure. The samples in the differential sensor were randomly split into two sets: 25 for the training set and 10 for the prediction set. The K value in the KNN model was set to 3, and the numbers of PCs selected in each PCA models were 3, 3, 4, 3, 2, respectively. The results of the two supervised pattern recognition models showed a prediction ability of 100% (see Table 2). On the basis of the results, we can deduce that this differential sensor possessed good performance in discriminating these nucleobases.

This work describes the demonstration of a conceptually novel “synthesis-identification integration” strategy coupled with a multidimensional array of fluorescence spectral data to construct a multidimensional differential fluorescence sensing platform for the discrimination of five different nitrogen-containing nucleobases, namely, adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine, or uracil. It was observed that sodium citrate could react with each nucleobase to produce different nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) under hydrothermal conditions. The CDs were confirmed by using various techniques such as transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Each CDs displayed a characteristic fluorescence spectral pattern as a fingerprint for classification and identification. Three different fluorescence data sets of the CDs, namely, single-wavelength excitation fluorescence spectral data, single-wavelength emission fluorescence spectral data, and fluorescence Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs), were respectively submitted to principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA). The results showed that the discrimination ability for nucleobase using EEM data set as input data excelled the other two. In addition, K-nearest neighbor (KNN) and soft independent modeling of class analogy (SIMCA) algorithms correctly identified the five nucleobases with a classification accuracy of 100%. The proposed “synthesis-identification integration” strategy together with a multidimensional array of fluorescence data simplifies operation steps and avoids the use of multiple sensing elements or several devices. Future work will be performed to further validate the merit of the proposed strategy in multianalytes of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC−21305061), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20171BAB203018 and 20151BAB203021), the Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (GJJ160006 and GJJ160204), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC-201802), and the State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (SKLCBC−2013010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.04.019.
